title: Slice: Extract Middle Portion
description: Given an array, return a new array containing elements from the second element (index 1) up to, but not including, the last element.
whatShouldHappen: End index should be the array length minus 1.
patternExplanation: .slice(start, end) extracts a section of an array without modifying the original.

title: Slice: Clone Array
description: Given an array, return a perfect shallow clone of it using the `.slice()` method. The new array should have the same elements but a different memory reference.
whatShouldHappen: This default behavior extracts the entire array from index 0 to the end.
patternExplanation: Calling .slice() with no arguments is a classic way to create a shallow copy of an array.

title: Slice: Take Last N
description: Given an array and a number `n`, return a new array containing the last `n` elements. Use a single negative index with `.slice()`.
whatShouldHappen: Call .slice() passing `-n` as the first argument.
Do not provide a second argument.
patternExplanation: A negative start index in .slice() counts from the end of the array, making it perfect for 'take' operations.

title: Slice: All But Last
description: Given an array, return a new array containing all elements except the very last one. Use a negative *end* index.
whatShouldHappen: Pass -1 as the second argument (the end index).
patternExplanation: Just like the start index, the end index can be negative to indicate a position relative to the end of the array.

title: Slice: Simple Pagination
description: Implement a simple pagination helper. Given an array of items, a `pageNumber` (1-indexed), and a `pageSize`, return the items for that specific page.
patternExplanation: Pagination is essentially taking a fixed-size window from a larger dataset using slice.

title: Slice: Get File Extension
description: Given a filename string (e.g., 'image.png'), return the extension part (e.g., 'png'). Use `.split('')` to turn the string into an array, then use `.lastIndexOf('.')` and `.slice()` to extract the extension. (Normally we'd use string methods, but practice your array slicing here!)
whatShouldHappen: Convert filename string to an array of characters.
If no '.' exists, return an empty string.
Slice from (dotIndex + 1) to the end of the array.
Join the sliced array back into a string and return it.
patternExplanation: Combining search methods like lastIndexOf with extraction methods like slice is a fundamental string/array processing pattern.

title: Slice: Take First N
description: Given an array and a number `n`, return a new array containing the first `n` elements using `.slice()`.
whatShouldHappen: Pass 0 as the start index and `n` as the end index.
patternExplanation: Slice(0, n) is the idiomatic way to 'take' the beginning of a collection.

title: Slice: Rotate Array
description: Given an array and a number `k`, rotate the array to the left by `k` positions. For example, `[1, 2, 3]` rotated left by 1 becomes `[2, 3, 1]`. Use `.slice()` and `.concat()` to return a NEW array. Do not mutate the original.
whatShouldHappen: Normalize `k` by using `k % arr.length` (in case k is larger than the array).
Extract the first part (elements to be moved to the end) using .slice(0, k).
Extract the second part (elements that stay at the front) using .slice(k).
Return the second part concatenated with the first part.
patternExplanation: Slice and concat allow you to split and rebuild an array in a different order without dangerous mutations.

title: Slice: Exact Middle
description: Given an array, return a NEW array containing only the middle element (if length is odd) or the middle two elements (if length is even). Use `.slice()`.
whatShouldHappen: Calculate the start index based on whether length is odd or even.
For odd length `L`, start is `floor(L / 2)` and end is `start + 1`.
For even length `L`, start is `(L / 2) - 1` and end is `start + 2`.
patternExplanation: Slice's start and end logic must be carefully calculated when dealing with center-based extractions.

title: Slice: Remove at Index (Immutable)
description: Given an array and an index, return a NEW array with the element at that index removed. Use `.slice()` and `.concat()` (or spread). Do not use `.splice()` as it mutates the original.
whatShouldHappen: Slice the part *before* the index: .slice(0, index).
patternExplanation: Combining slice(0, i) and slice(i + 1) is the standard immutable pattern for deleting an item from an array.
